summary: Justin Brennan conducts a live walkthrough of how to analyze a multifamily deal using a real Dallas property example from Crexy. He demonstrates using the broker's offering memorandum, extracting data into a deal analyzer, adjusting conservative assumptions, backing into maximum offer price, writing an LOI, and preparing a pitch deck for investors.
takeaways: Use deal analyzer to input unit mix, rents, and expenses directly from broker OM (offering memorandum)
Separate contract services (pool, landscaping, pest control, cleaning) from repairs/maintenance line items—brokers combine them to hide true costs
Remove management fees from general administrative expenses and input as separate 6% line item
Add reserve/replacement line of $250-$300 per unit per year for capital expenditures—brokers omit this entirely
Set minimum 10% economic loss/vacancy assumption, never believe broker's 5%—"there's no way you're operating less than 10% in Texas"
Target expense ratio of 45-55% for buildings built 1950-1980; 35-45% for 1980-2000; 30s or lower for post-2000 buildings
Debt coverage ratio (DCR) must be 1.25 or greater for lender approval—this is the critical constraint
Back into max offer price using required metrics: 14-20% IRR, 6-10% cash-on-cash, 1.8x equity multiple minimum
Use Chat GPT to verify property taxes by county and market value—can save 30-40 minutes of research
Write LOI at 1% earnest money deposit (non-binding, refundable until due diligence), shoot out multiple offers—"you could spit these out 400 a day"
Leverage broker relationships by offering professional deal analysis and saying 'show me where I'm wrong'—positions you for pre-market deal flow

skill_candidates.change: Extract and separate cost categories correctly from broker P&L before inputting into analyzer
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin shows how brokers hide true operating costs by combining contract services with repairs/maintenance; demonstrates pulling out pool service, landscaping, pest control, and management fees into separate line items to reveal actual 63% expense ratio vs. broker's hidden allocation

skill_candidates.change: Apply 10% minimum economic loss assumption instead of accepting broker's 5% vacancy projection
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin repeatedly states 'there's no way on planet Earth you're operating a property in Texas for anything less than 10% economic loss' and explains brokers cherry-pick best-case scenarios; this adjustment directly impacts DCR and max offer price

skill_candidates.change: Back into maximum offer price using target metrics rather than accepting asking price
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin demonstrates taking a property listed at unknown price, plugging in corrected assumptions, and determining max offer of $1.6M-$1.8M based on 15% IRR, 6-10% cash-on-cash, 1.25+ DCR requirements; shows working backwards from required return metrics

skill_candidates.change: Use initial low LOI offers as rapport-building conversation tools, not binding commitments
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin explains LOI is 'non-binding,' 'means nothing,' and can submit '400 a day'; advises positioning offer as data-driven analysis ('show me where I'm wrong') to transition conversations to off-market deal flow behind the scenes

title: Weekly Coaching_05-15-25 | Justin Brennan
